revision.comment: initial text of stub article -- 1st steps in a constructive direction
+ Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture
revision.text:
{{under construction}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = Minasejingu02s2000.jpg | width = | caption = | type = Imperial Shrine | dedication = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jinjū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Viscissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Viciissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/186605327 OCLC 186605327] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[ja:水無瀬神宮]]

revision.comment: + Horton
revision.text:
{{under construction}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = Minase_jingu.jpg | width = | caption = Front view | type = Imperial Shrine | dedication = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded = 1240 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor -- each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Daigo was banished to [[Oki Island]]. Tsuchimikado was banished to [[Tosa province]] or [[Tosa Domain]], now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]; and then to [[Awa]]. Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]]. In 1873, the ''kami'' of the Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined in 1874; and the ''kami'' of the JUntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA273&dq=minase+jingu&lr=&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=minase%20jingu&f=false ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jinjū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Viscissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Viciissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/186605327 OCLC 186605327] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[ja:水無瀬神宮]]

revision.text:
{{under construction}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = Minase_jingu.jpg | width = | caption = Front view | type = Imperial Shrine | dedication = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded = 1240 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor -- each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [http://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104&dq= ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [http://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186&dq= ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193.. In 1873, the ''kami'' of t he Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined in 1874; and the ''kami'' of the JUntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA273&dq=minase+jingu&lr=&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=minase%20jingu&f=false ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jinjū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. [http://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186&dq= ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193.. In 1873, the ''kami'' of t he Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined in 1874; and the ''kami'' of the JUntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA273&dq=minase+jingu&lr=&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=minase%20jingu&f=false ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jinjū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Viscissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. ISBN 0-88920-997-9 * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Viciissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/186605327 OCLC 186605327] * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. 10-ISBN 0-415-32379-7/13-ISBN 978-0-415-32379-6 [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[ja:水無瀬神宮]]

(1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor -- each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [http://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104&dq= ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [http://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186&dq= ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193.. In 1873, the ''kami'' of t he Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined in 1874; and the ''kami'' of the JUntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA273&dq=minase+jingu&lr=&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=minase%20jingu&f=false ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jinjū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Viscissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. ISBN 0-88920-997-9 * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. London: Taylor & Francis. 10-ISBN 0-415-32379-7/13-ISBN 978-0-415-32379-6 [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[ja:水無瀬神宮]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = Minase_jingu.jpg | width = | caption = Front view | type = Imperial Shrine | dedication = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded = 1240 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [http://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104&dq= ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [http://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186&dq= ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193.. In 1873, the ''kami'' of t he Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined in 1874; and the ''kami'' of the JUntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA273&dq=minase+jingu&lr=&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=minase%20jingu&f=false ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jinjū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Viscissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. ISBN 0-88920-997-9 * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Viciissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/186605327 OCLC 186605327] * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. 10-ISBN 0-415-32379-7/13-ISBN 978-0-415-32379-6 {{coord missing|Japan}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[ja:水無瀬神宮]]

''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jinjū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Viscissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. ISBN 0-88920-997-9 * Holton, Daniel Clarence. [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/186605327 OCLC 186605327] * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. 10-ISBN 0-415-32379-7/13-ISBN 978-0-415-32379-6 {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[ja:水無瀬神宮]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = Minase_jingu.jpg | width = | caption = Front view | type = Imperial Shrine | dedication = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded = 1240 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [http://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104&dq= ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [http://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186&dq= ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193.. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined in 1874; and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA273&dq=minase+jingu&lr=&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=minase%20jingu&f=false ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. ISBN 0-88920-997-9 * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/186605327 OCLC 186605327] * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. 10-ISBN 0-415-32379-7/13-ISBN 978-0-415-32379-6 {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[ja:水無瀬神宮]] [[zh:水無瀨神宮]]

This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. ISBN 0-88920-997-9 * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/186605327 OCLC 186605327] * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. 10-ISBN 0-415-32379-7/13-ISBN 978-0-415-32379-6 {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[fr:Minase-jingū]] [[ja:水無瀬神宮]] [[zh:水無瀨神宮]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = Minase_jingu.jpg | width = | caption = Front view | type = Imperial Shrine | dedication = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded = 1240 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [http://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104&dq= ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [http://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186&dq= ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA273&dq=minase+jingu&lr=&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=minase%20jingu&f=false ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. ISBN 0-88920-997-9 * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/186605327 OCLC 186605327] * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. 10-ISBN 0-415-32379-7/13-ISBN 978-0-415-32379-6 {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[fr:Minase-jingū]] [[ja:水無瀬神宮]] [[zh:水無瀨神宮]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = Minase_jingu.jpg | width = | caption = Front view | type = Imperial Shrine | dedication = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded = 1240 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [http://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104&dq= ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [http://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186&dq= ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA273&dq=minase+jingu&lr=&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=minase%20jingu&f=false ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. ISBN 0-88920-997-9 * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/186605327 OCLC 186605327] * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. 10-ISBN 0-415-32379-7/13-ISBN 978-0-415-32379-6 {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]

(1922). (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. ISBN 0-88920-997-9 * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/186605327 OCLC 186605327] * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = Minase_jingu.jpg | width = | caption = Front view | type = Imperial Shrine | dedication = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded = 1240 | closed = | founder = | priest = | location_map = Japan | coordinates ={{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104&dq= ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186&dq= ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA273&dq=minase+jingu&lr=&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=minase%20jingu&f=false ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA273&dq=minase+jingu&lr=&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=minase%20jingu&f=false ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = Minasejingu02bs5s2010.jpg | caption = Front view | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Imperial Shrine | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104&dq= ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186&dq= ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA273&dq=minase+jingu&lr=&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=minase%20jingu&f=false ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]]

revision.comment: Moving [[:Category:Beppo Shrines]] to [[:Category:Beppyo shrines]] per [[Wikipedia:Categories for discussion/Log/2022 June 2#Category:Beppo Shrines]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}}[[Category:Beppyo shrines]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = Minasejingu02bs5s2010.jpg | caption = Front view | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Imperial Shrine | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104&dq= ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186&dq= ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA273&dq=minase+jingu&lr=&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=minase%20jingu&f=false ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Kanpo-taisya]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Imperial Shrine | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa Province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. 水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Imperial Shrine | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]]

(1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963).
