{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia20]] [[en:Minase Jingu]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia05]] [[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia05]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia20]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Minase Jingu]]

''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:ファイル]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]]

水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]]

This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]]

[http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:Pages of u]][[Category:ginality]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category:qq 1]]\[[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:if these are dates then move them to completion tier 5]][[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:if these are dates then move them to completion tier 5]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:Pages of u]][[Category:ginality]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category:qq 1]]\[[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:Pages of u]][[Category:ginality]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category:qq 1]]\[[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:New]]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 1]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:11]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:11]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:Pages of u]][[Category:ginality]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:11]] [[Category:tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]]\[[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:11]] [[Category:tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:11]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]][[en:Minase Jingu]][[Category:11]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:ginality]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:11]] [[Category:tier 2]]\[[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:11]] [[Category:tier 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Minase Jingu]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:100 oldest pages]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:ginality]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[sn:Minase Jingu]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[sn:Minase Jingu]]
