{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] }} [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[sn:Minase Jingu]]

''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Cgoodbye $1 2]] }} [[sn:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Go-Toba]], [[Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]]

In the struggle with the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[:en:Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], [[:en:Emperor Tsuchimikado|Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[:en:Emperor Juntoku|Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], [[:en:Emperor Tsuchimikado|Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[:en:Emperor Juntoku|Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[:en:Kōchi Prefecture|Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[:en:Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] }} [[sn:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], [[:en:Emperor Tsuchimikado|Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[:en:Emperor Juntoku|Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], [[:en:Emperor Tsuchimikado|Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[:en:Emperor Juntoku|Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[Tosa province]] (now known as [[:en:Kōchi Prefecture|Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[:en:Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Jingū|List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[:en:National Geographic Society|National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] }} [[sn:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[:en:Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Jingū|List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] }} [[sn:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], [[:en:Emperor Tsuchimikado|Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[:en:Emperor Juntoku|Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[:en:Shimamoto, Osaka|Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], [[:en:Emperor Tsuchimikado|Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[:en:Emperor Juntoku|Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[:en:Oki Island|Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[:en:Tosa province|Tosa province]] (now known as [[:en:Kōchi Prefecture|Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[:en:Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[:en:Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] }} [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:Jingus without interwikis]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], [[:en:Emperor Tsuchimikado|Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[:en:Emperor Juntoku|Emperor Juntoku]] | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[:en:Shimamoto, Osaka|Shimamoto, Osaka]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], [[:en:Emperor Tsuchimikado|Emperor Tsuchimikado]] and [[:en:Emperor Juntoku|Emperor Juntoku]]. In the struggle with the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[:en:Oki Island|Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[:en:Tosa province|Tosa province]] (now known as [[:en:Kōchi Prefecture|Kōchi Prefecture]]); and later, he removed himself to [[:en:Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa province]], where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at [[:en:Sado, Niigata|Sado Island]].Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Jingū|List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: [[:en:National Geographic Society|National Geographic Society]]. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] }} [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:Jingus without interwikis]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba}}, {{ill|Emperor Tsuchimikado|en|Emperor Tsuchimikado|WD=Q357269|lt=Emperor Tsuchimikado}} and {{ill|Emperor Juntoku|en|Emperor Juntoku|WD=Q357249|lt=Emperor Juntoku}} | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in {{ill|Shimamoto, Osaka|en|Shimamoto, Osaka|WD=Q1347149|lt=Shimamoto, Osaka}}Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba}}, {{ill|Emperor Tsuchimikado|en|Emperor Tsuchimikado|WD=Q357269|lt=Emperor Tsuchimikado}} and {{ill|Emperor Juntoku|en|Emperor Juntoku|WD=Q357249|lt=Emperor Juntoku}}. In the struggle with the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[:en:Oki Island|Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[:en:Tosa province|Tosa province]] (now known as {{ill|Kōchi Prefecture|en|Kōchi Prefecture|WD=Q134093|lt=Kōchi Prefecture}}); and later, he removed himself to {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|en|Awa Province (Tokushima)|WD=Q907457|lt=Awa province}}, where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at {{ill|Sado, Niigata|en|Sado, Niigata|WD=Q499047|lt=Sado Island}}.Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * {{ill|List of Jingū|en|List of Jingū|WD=Q120680118|lt=List of Jingū}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|WD=Q7883225|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)}} ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: {{ill|National Geographic Society|en|National Geographic Society|WD=Q167186|lt=National Geographic Society}}. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane}}, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] }} [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:Jingus without interwikis]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

(1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[:en:Tosa province|Tosa province]] (now known as {{ill|Kōchi Prefecture|en|Kōchi Prefecture|WD=Q134093|lt=Kōchi Prefecture|1=Kōchi Prefecture}}); and later, he removed himself to {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|en|Awa Province (Tokushima)|WD=Q907457|lt=Awa province|1=Awa Province (Tokushima)}}, where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at {{ill|Sado, Niigata|en|Sado, Niigata|WD=Q499047|lt=Sado Island|1=Sado, Niigata}}.Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * {{ill|List of Jingū|en|List of Jingū|WD=Q120680118|lt=List of Jingū|1=List of Jingū}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|WD=Q7883225|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)}} ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: {{ill|National Geographic Society|en|National Geographic Society|WD=Q167186|lt=National Geographic Society|1=National Geographic Society}}. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] }} [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:Jingus without interwikis]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] }} [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:Jingus without interwikis]] [[Category:Pages without wikidata]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Minase Shrine
水無瀬神宮 | image = 211120 Minase-jingu Shimamoto Osaka pref Japan07s3.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.8849|N|135.673|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]] | deity = {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|qid=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba|1=Emperor Go-Toba}}, {{ill|Emperor Tsuchimikado|en|Emperor Tsuchimikado|qid=Q357269|lt=Emperor Tsuchimikado|1=Emperor Tsuchimikado}} and {{ill|Emperor Juntoku|en|Emperor Juntoku|qid=Q357249|lt=Emperor Juntoku|1=Emperor Juntoku}} | founded_by = | established = 1240 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Minase Shrine'''|水無瀬神宮|Minase jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in {{ill|Shimamoto, Osaka|en|Shimamoto, Osaka|qid=Q1347149|lt=Shimamoto, Osaka|1=Shimamoto, Osaka}}Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|qid=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba|1=Emperor Go-Toba}}, {{ill|Emperor Tsuchimikado|en|Emperor Tsuchimikado|qid=Q357269|lt=Emperor Tsuchimikado|1=Emperor Tsuchimikado}} and {{ill|Emperor Juntoku|en|Emperor Juntoku|qid=Q357249|lt=Emperor Juntoku|1=Emperor Juntoku}}. In the struggle with the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|qid=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, the three historical figures are united by one common factor—each was overpowered and banished from the Imperial center in Kyoto: Go-Toba was banished to [[:en:Oki Island|Oki Island]], where he died.Brownlee, John S. (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=P1OZUmnLTdoC&pg=PA104 ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712),'' p.104.] Tsuchimikado felt compelled to abandon Kyoto, traveling first to [[:en:Tosa province|Tosa province]] (now known as {{ill|Kōchi Prefecture|en|Kōchi Prefecture|qid=Q134093|lt=Kōchi Prefecture|1=Kōchi Prefecture}}); and later, he removed himself to {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|en|Awa Province (Tokushima)|qid=Q907457|lt=Awa province|1=Awa Province (Tokushima)}}, where he died in exile.Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2ujfVq9v4zoC&pg=PA186 ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1, p. 186.] Juntoku was forced to end his days at {{ill|Sado, Niigata|en|Sado, Niigata|qid=Q499047|lt=Sado Island|1=Sado, Niigata}}.Bornoff, Nicholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan,'' p. 193. In 1873, the ''kami'' of Go-Daigo and Tushimikado were enshrined, and the ''kami'' of Juntoku was enshrined in 1874.Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SRwNAAAAYAAJ&dq=minase+jingu&pg=PA273 ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan,'' p. 273.] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Minase''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Minase's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Minase ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * {{ill|List of Jingū|en|List of Jingū|qid=Q120680118|lt=List of Jingū|1=List of Jingū}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|qid=Q7883225|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)}} ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * Bornoff, Niholas. (2005). ''National Geographic Traveler Japan.'' Washington, D.C.: {{ill|National Geographic Society|en|National Geographic Society|qid=Q167186|lt=National Geographic Society|1=National Geographic Society}}. * Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9}} * Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). ''The Political Philosophy of Modern Shintō, a Study of the State Religion of Japan.'' Chicago: University of Chicago Libraries. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2857479 OCLC 2857479] * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|qid=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} * Takekoshi, Yosaburō. (2004). ''The Economic Aspects of the History of the Civilization of Japan,'' Volume 1. London: Taylor & Francis. {{ISBN|978-0-415-32379-6}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] }} [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[en:Minase Jingu]] [[Category:Pages without wikidata]]
